original_abstract: Building upon the theological tensions developed in Romans 7:7–25, Romans 8:3–4 frame the "incapability of the law" as a pivotal component of a broader crisis of sin requiring divine intervention. This study argues that the opening clause of Romans 8:3 (τὸ ... ἀδύνατον) is best understood not as a grammatical anacoluthon, as is frequently suggested, but as an accusative of respect accompanied by dependent clauses. By adopting this syntactical lens, it becomes clear that Paul identifies two secondary issues—the weakness of the flesh and the resulting impotence of the law—as the primary frames of reference for his claims in this passage. Consequently, Romans 8:3–4 presents a comprehensive resolution to this threefold problem. Specifically, Paul contends that through the incarnation and mission of God’s Son, the dominion of sin and the frailty of the flesh are overcome by the condemnation of sin in Christ’s own flesh. This act achieves a dual purpose: it eradicates the law's inability to justify by ensuring that the law's righteous requirements are fulfilled through Christ.
humanized_abstract: Continuing to exploit the theological tensions of Romans 7:7–25, the ‘helplessness of the law’ becomes a key element of the total crisis of sin that demands divine action. This study argues that the clause τὸ ... ἀδύνατον in Romans 8:3 is an accusative of respect with dependent clauses rather than being an anacoluthon as commonly supposed. Paul shows that the secondary consequences of the crisis in Romans 8 — the helplessness of the flesh and the inability of the law — are the dominant references for his statements in the period. Romans 8:3–4 then represents Paul’s complete resolution of the three aspects of the crisis in sin. There, in the Son of God, through his incarnation and mission, God acts so that sin has ceased to be the reigning power of life and so that the power of the human flesh is annihilated because sin in the flesh of the Son has been condemned. Through this Christ is able to nullify the powerlessness of the law in that by the flesh of the Son, in whom God’s law resides, the law’s just requirements are fully vindicated.
